title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
The 50-30-20 Rule
This is an efficient rule that works on the basis of dividing your income to meet your expenses in an organized manner. In a perfect world, if you can manage it, you should stick to the following division of your total in-hand income:
For Need-based Expenses (groceries, EMI, rent, etc.) - 50%
For Wants-based Expenses (holidays, entertainment, shopping sprees,etc.) - 30%
For Savings (mutual funds, equity, FDs, debt, etc.) - 20%
Say you earn a salary, in-hand, of Rs. 2 lakh. Your need-based expenses will amount to Rs. 1 lakh straight away. The rest can be divided into desire-based expenses and savings. However, you should never compromise on the savings part. The least you should aim to save out of your earnings is 20%. If you save more, then that’s a bonus. Furthermore, as your income increases, your requirements may not increase in the same proportion. Hence, you may have surplus funds that you can invest. For instance, when you are young, you may have rental expenses, but once you buy a house when you are older (assuming your loan is paid up), your rental expenses are non-existent.
